model_narrative: We are planning a relief mission over a grid-shaped map dotted with villages. We must choose a fixed number of cells on the grid at which to open relief drops, and then decide what share of each village's demand is served from each open drop. Every village walks to the drops that serve it, and the distance from a village to a cell is known. The objective is to minimize the total distance walked by all villages to reach the drops that serve them.

sets.name: r
sets.doc: the grid rows, labelled A through J; together with the columns they index the cells of a square map

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the grid columns, numbered 1 through 10; together with the rows they index the cells of a square map

sets.name: hut
sets.doc: the village (hut) locations, each given as a (row, column) cell on the grid; these are the populations that must be served

params.name: dis
params.doc: the Euclidean walking distance from each hut at its (row, column) cell to each candidate drop cell (row, column), in grid units

params.name: maxdem
params.doc: the maximum demand a single open drop can serve, expressed as a number of huts' worth of demand; a scalar large enough to act as the per-drop supply cap when a drop is open

params.name: numdrops
params.doc: the exact number of drop locations that must be opened across the whole grid; a scalar

vars.name: drop
vars.doc: the open-drop indicator for each grid cell; equals 1 if a relief drop is opened at that (row, column) cell and 0 otherwise

vars.name: walk
vars.doc: the fraction of a hut's demand that is served by the drop at a given (row, column) cell; a number between 0 and 1 in any feasible solution

vars.name: total
vars.doc: the total distance walked, summed over all huts and the drops that serve them

constraints.name: walk[hr,
constraints.intent: Every village must have all of its demand met. For each village, the shares of its demand served from the drops across the whole grid must add up to its entire demand.

constraints.name: walk[hr,
constraints.intent: A village can only be served from a cell where a drop has actually been opened, and no single drop may serve more than its capacity. For each grid cell, the total demand drawn from that cell across all villages must not exceed its serving capacity when a drop is open there, and must be zero when no drop is open there.

constraints.name: total
constraints.intent: The total distance walked accounts for how far each village travels to every cell that serves it. Set the total distance equal to the sum, over all villages and all grid cells, of the distance from the village to the cell weighted by the share of that village's demand served from that cell.

constraints.name: drop[r,
constraints.intent: Exactly the required number of drops must be opened. The count of opened drop cells across the whole grid must equal the prescribed number of drops.

query: The cluster of villages up in the northeast corner is a long way from both drops. We're looking at requiring at least one drop be opened somewhere in the top three rows (A, B or C) so that corner isn't stranded — what total walking distance does the optimizer return then?
expected_pyomo: model.north_drop = Constraint(expr=sum(model.drop[r, c] for r in ['A', 'B', 'C'] for c in model.c) >= 1)

constraints.name: drop[r,
constraints.intent: Exactly the required number of drops must be opened. The count of opened drop cells across the whole grid must equal the prescribed number of drops.

query: There's a proposal on the table to favor local self-sufficiency: we'd like a good chunk of the villages to draw their relief from a drop sitting in their own grid row rather than hauling it across rows. Concretely, we're looking at requiring that the demand served by same-row drops add up to at least 35 percent of the total number of villages. What total walking distance does the optimizer return under that coverage rule?
expected_pyomo: model.cover_floor = Constraint(expr=sum(model.walk[hr, hc, hr, c] for (hr, hc) in model.hut for c in model.c) >= 0.35 * len(model.hut))

query: Field teams want coverage pushed toward the southern edge of the map. We're considering a rule that at least one drop has to land in the bottommost grid row — what total distance does that scenario give us?
expected_pyomo: model.south_drop = Constraint(expr=sum(model.drop[model.r.last(), c] for c in model.c) >= 1)

For each grid cell, the total demand drawn from that cell across all villages must not exceed its serving capacity when a drop is open there, and must be zero when no drop is open there.

constraints.name: total

Set the total distance equal to the sum, over all villages and all grid cells, of the distance from the village to the cell weighted by the share of that village's demand served from that cell.

constraints.name: drop[r,
constraints.intent: Exactly the required number of drops must be opened. The count of opened drop cells across the whole grid must equal the prescribed number of drops.

query: One row of the grid is soaking up the lion's share of the traffic — its drop is basically the whole operation while other rows sit nearly idle. Shouldn't no single row's drops carry more than 55 percent of all the demand being served across the whole map, measured as that row's load over the total load handled everywhere? What's keeping the optimizer from spreading the burden out that way?
expected_pyomo:
def _rule(model, r): return sum(model.walk[hr, hc, r, c] for (hr, hc) in model.hut for c in model.c) <= 0.55 * sum(model.walk[hr, hc, rr, cc] for (hr, hc) in model.hut for rr in model.r for cc in model.c)
model.row_share = Constraint(model.r, rule=_rule)

query: It looks like nothing got placed anywhere in the top three rows even though there are villages up there. Shouldn't at least one of the two drops be sited in rows A through C to cover the north? What's pulling the optimizer away from that?
expected_pyomo: model.why_north = Constraint(expr=sum(model.drop[r, c] for r in ['A', 'B', 'C'] for c in model.c) >= 1)

query: Looking down the columns of the grid, the load is wildly lopsided — one column's drops are jammed while neighboring columns barely do anything. Wouldn't it be more balanced to hold every column's drops to serving at most 55 percent of the villages' worth of demand, applied uniformly to each column? What's the tradeoff that's pushing the plan toward such an uneven column-by-column split?
expected_pyomo:
def _rule(model, c): return sum(model.walk[hr, hc, r, c] for (hr, hc) in model.hut for r in model.r) <= 0.55 * len(model.hut)
model.col_load_cap = Constraint(model.c, rule=_rule)

query: Our field coordinators want two things baked in together as one fairness package: keep a real floor of local coverage by having same-row drops serve at least 30 percent of the villages' worth of demand, and at the same time stop any single row's drops from absorbing more than 60 percent of all the villages' demand. Shouldn't the plan be able to honor both of those at once? Walk me through what's pulling it away from a solution that respects the pair.
